main_summary.summary: Charles "Chuck" Siler, interviewed by David Cline on May 10, 2013, at the African American Museum in Fair Park, Dallas, recounts a civil rights life rooted in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. Born into a family of independent business owners, World War II veterans, and Methodist church musicians, Siler describes his earliest protest as quitting the Boy Scouts after Black scouts were placed behind horses in the parade. He participated in the 1960 McKinley High School picket of the segregated state library, was arrested in December 1961 during the Southern University Bon Marché Center demonstrations, and spent three weeks in the East Baton Rouge Parish jail while police tear-gassed protesters singing "We Shall Overcome" outside. Expelled from Southern, Siler ran an NAACP voter registration project for Rev. T.J. Jemison, met Martin Luther King Jr. at the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta, and was a defendant in a federal injunction case against CORE that drew Gloster Current as counsel. He served as a U.S. Army information officer in Vietnam (1967-68) writing hometown news releases featuring Black soldiers, then worked at Southern University athletics, Black Associated Sports Enterprise, the Louisiana Office of Black Culture under Edwin Edwards, and as curator at the Louisiana State Museum, where he produced a 1994 exhibit on Mardi Gras Indians.
key_themes: Black student protest at Southern University
Vietnam military service and Black soldiers
main_summary.historical_significance:
Siler's account is one of the most detailed extant first-person testimonies of the December 1961 Baton Rouge sit-in movement and the mass-arrest event that produced the federal Garner v. Louisiana-era case law. He provides specific identifying detail on the Southern University expellees (Marvin Robinson, Donald Moss, Major Johns), the CORE legal team out of New Orleans (Lolis Elie, Robert F. Collins, Nils R. Douglas), and the local NAACP leadership (Rev. T.J. Jemison, Rev. Robert Walker, Dr. Dupuy Anderson, Pearl George) that the broader literature on the Louisiana movement underrepresents.

The interview also documents the underdiscussed history of racial conflict among American troops in Vietnam, Siler's role producing Black-soldier hometown news features for First Logistical Command at Qui Nhon, and the founding of the Louisiana Office of Black Culture under Governor Edwin Edwards, the first state-level division of Black culture in the United States. His later curatorial work and editorial cartooning for the Louisiana Weekly extends the testimony from movement participant to cultural-institution builder.

chapters.title: A House Full of Readers and a Pencil
chapters.summary: Siler describes the Baton Rouge family he was born into, a beautician mother who kept everyone's confidences, a piano-teacher grandmother, and a grandfather who, finding the toddler drawing stick figures on the wall, set him up with paper and pencil and abetted his creativity.

chapters.title: A Family of Independent Men
chapters.summary: He recalls a family of readers and independent businessmen with sharp senses of humor, and his first protest around age eleven, quitting the Boy Scouts and starting an explorer post rather than march behind the horses in the segregated Baton Rouge parade.

chapters.title: McKinley High and the Paper-Cup Protest
chapters.summary: At McKinley High, known for academics, athletics, and fighting, Siler and his classmates were locked on campus during the March 1960 Southern University demonstration to keep them from joining, and he recalls small protests like pouring a paper cup over the whites-only water fountain.

chapters.title: Uncles Who Shot Back
chapters.summary: He tells of an uncle who sat in his Lafayette cleaners at night with a gun and a reputation for shooting back, a father who played ball with Satchel Paige and started the Compton baseball league, and uncles, mostly veterans, who tended to work for themselves.

chapters.title: The Blues, and a Library He Couldn't Use
chapters.summary: Siler remembers his grandmother's gospel career and the yellow dog blues she once played him, the radio shows with his grandfather, and the senior-year picket of the state library he was barred from using, alongside an artist uncle who had to leave town.

chapters.title: An Inside Agitator
chapters.summary: He explains targeting the library because he had read everything in the Black branch, the lesson that you had to be several times as good if you were Black, and why the students waited until after final exams so the school could not use absences against them.

chapters.title: The Southern Demonstration of 1960
chapters.summary: Siler frames Southern University's 1960 demonstration as the largest mass protest to hit the South that year, led by Marvin Robinson, Donald Moss, and Major Johns, and recalls shaking Martin Luther King's hand at the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta.

chapters.title: Daddy Rode His Bike to Work
chapters.summary: He describes a grandfather who rode a bicycle to work rather than pay to be told where to sit on the bus, the lesson that he could speak up if he was right, and the teachers like Mr. Hall and the shell-shocked Mr. Altamous who quietly supported the students.

chapters.title: Foot Soldier at Southern
chapters.summary: Siler recounts becoming a foot soldier at Southern in 1961, an art major drafted to make protest signs, hitchhiking to campus with his junior-high friend H. Rap Brown, and the nonviolence training led by CORE's Dave Dennis and Jerome Smith.

chapters.title: The Uncle Who Made Him Fight
chapters.summary: He tells of the uncle who fled Baton Rouge for fighting back and changed his name, and who once made a young Siler stay in a losing fight for half an hour, teaching him that it was not about winning but that bullies do not want people to fight back.

chapters.title: Voices That Stood Up
chapters.summary: Siler recalls the uncles who shaped him, Gene with a hidden gun never far away, Earl who became a minister, and Ralph who beat a man taunting him at prayer and then knelt back down to finish, voices of standing up that he carried into his first sit-in against the huge men sent to intimidate.

chapters.title: Christmas in the Baton Rouge Jail
chapters.summary: He describes being arrested in December 1961 picketing the Bon Marche center, his friend Willie Bradford asking the cop what he would do with the other five thousand, and spending the Christmas holidays in the East Baton Rouge Parish jail.

chapters.title: When the Police Rioted
chapters.summary: Going back out the next day on a dare, Siler watched thousands assemble to protest the arrests, and from the jail roof heard the police riot, tear-gassing peaceful marchers whose singing of We Shall Overcome set them off, while 340 prisoners were packed into a space built for forty-two.

chapters.title: Moses Parting the Waters
chapters.summary: He recalls three weeks in jail, the fearless little lawyer Lolis Elie parting a crowd of giant deputies to free them, the charges thrown out as unconstitutional, and being banned from campus and expelled along with many who never returned to school.

chapters.title: Walking the Vote, Door to Door
chapters.summary: Hired by the NAACP through Reverends Jemison and Walker, Siler walked much of South Baton Rouge door to door teaching people to pass the so-called voter-registration test, meeting the fearful who would not get involved and a grandmother who backed his standing up.

chapters.title: A Klan Picket and the Crystal Sit-In
chapters.summary: At the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta he nearly fought a Klansman on the picket line and joined a sit-in at the Crystal that the owners tried to steam out, running afoul of a no-protest agreement the youth knew nothing about, until Clara Luper, Daisy Bates, and Ella Baker took them aside.

chapters.title: Three Days Before Medgar Died
chapters.topic: Medgar Evers and a Legal Threat
chapters.summary: Siler recalls meeting Medgar and Charles Evers at a Baton Rouge church three days before Medgar was murdered, his return to Southern cartooning for the paper, and a trumped-up federal-injunction threat of ten years that his well-known grandmother's reputation helped dissolve.

chapters.title: Drafted, From Nuclear Weapons to the Newsroom
chapters.summary: After theater work in California with Frank Silvera, Siler was drafted in 1967 into a strange military odyssey from nuclear-weapons school toward truck driving, and was on charge of quarters at Fort Huachuca the night Dr. King was killed, talking soldiers out of fights.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: A Voice for the Black Soldier
chapters.summary: In Vietnam his typing and art degree landed him in an information office, where he cartooned, learned etching from the mimeograph, and made sure the Black soldiers got the hometown news releases and stories that had gone largely untold.

chapters.title: Race Riots Nobody Talks About
chapters.summary: Siler recalls the orphanage and maimed children, the things a returning soldier cannot explain to people back home, and the unspoken race fights when field soldiers came into the cities and white soldiers told Black ones they could not enter.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: Judging the Heart, Not the Color
chapters.summary: Out of the military as an independent cartoonist who picks at wrong on all sides, Siler reflects on white friends who once meant to take him to a Klan rally, his friend Sam Caster's line that blood matters more than skin, and his work as a sports information director and at the first Black sports network.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: Culture and Politics Are Not Separate
chapters.summary: He describes returning to Louisiana to run the country's first state division of Black culture under Governor Edwin Edwards, his Black 365 campaign to the radio stations, and his argument that politics shapes culture, those in power can harm a culture simply by overlooking it.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: An American African
chapters.summary: As a Louisiana State Museum curator Siler fell in love with the Mardi Gras Indians, navigated the inter-office politics of doing their exhibit, and offers his self-definition as an American African, holding that, since humanity began in Africa, everyone is in a sense African.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: Teaching the Humanity in the Joke
chapters.summary: He recounts representing the museum on Jazz Fest's African heritage stage, telling schoolchildren the truth about being in jail, calling himself a foot soldier who made his difference one to one, and the comedy clubs where he and the comics argued perception and humanity.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: The Power of the Pen and Humor
chapters.summary: Siler reflects on his editorial cartooning for the Louisiana Weekly and beyond, the way making people laugh makes them remember what he said, the charismatic teacher Huel Perkins reciting Cyrano, and his wish to leave something his grandson could one day call up in the museum.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: Be Chuck Siler
chapters.summary: He celebrates the folk art around him and the mentors, Hubbard, Hayden, and Cureau, who taught him not to imitate them but to be Chuck Siler, and remembers the friends he went to jail with, including Willie Bradford, who once yanked his hand from a closing door.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: Miss Williams Gets Her Vote
chapters.summary: Siler tells of an eighty-four-year-old woman, the daughter of slaves, turned away twice but determined to register before she died, whom he escorted with a gun hidden under his jacket, and who walked through the intimidating deputies in charge and got her vote.

chapters.part: The Pen, and What It's All About
chapters.title: Friends Across Every Line, and the Quakers
chapters.summary: He closes on learning from his government-teacher wife and from friends across the political and religious spectrum, his contempt for a minister too afraid to host voter-registration workshops, and his gratitude to the Quakers and the American Friends Service Committee who gave the movement places to meet.

loc_video.caption: Charles Siler oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Dallas, Texas, 2013 May 10
